did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Gabor-filter-based Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for melanoma detection and classification in dermoscopy images. The method involves preprocessing skin images with Histogram Equalization and Gabor transforms, followed by feature extraction and classification using a CNN classifier. Performance metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) were obtained using this deep learning model on ISIC and SIIM-ISIC datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of a Gabor-filter-based Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for melanoma detection. It details specific methods (Histogram Equalization, Gabor transforms), datasets used (ISIC, SIIM-ISIC Kaggle), and reports new quantitative results (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) comparing the proposed model against existing methods, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Gabor Transform-Based Deep Learning System Using CNN for Melanoma Detection
brief_summary: This study proposes a Gabor transform-based Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) system for automated melanoma detection in dermoscopy images. The method preprocesses skin images using Histogram Equalization and Gabor transforms to extract multi-resolution texture features, which are then classified by the CNN. The model was evaluated on subsets of the ISIC and SIIM-ISIC Kaggle datasets, utilizing 1500 melanoma and 1000 non-melanoma images. Performance metrics included sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy, with the proposed method achieving 98.58% accuracy, 98.66% sensitivity, and 98.75% specificity on the ISIC dataset. The results demonstrate that the Gabor-filter-based approach outperforms several existing traditional and deep learning methods in melanoma classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for detection and classification. The study used 1500 melanoma images from the ISIC dataset and 1000 from the SIIM-ISIC Kaggle dataset for the experiment.

The negative class used for binary classification. The study used 1000 non-melanoma images from the ISIC dataset and 1000 from the SIIM-ISIC Kaggle dataset. Note: The paper uses 'non-melanoma' and 'healthy skin' interchangeably to denote the negative class, though 'non-melanoma skin cancer' is also used in the dataset description.

all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is to 'classify dermoscopy skin images as melanoma or non-melanoma' using a CNN classifier. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (malignant vs. benign/healthy) and reports binary classification metrics including accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity on the ISIC and SIIM-ISIC Kaggle datasets.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopy image dataset. The paper notes it was initially curated and overseen by the University Dermatology Centre in Muncie. Images in this dataset are 512 × 512 pixels.

use.training: 750 melanoma and 250 non-melanoma images were allocated for model training.
use.testing: 750 melanoma and 250 non-melanoma images were reserved for testing.

datasets_used.name: SIIM-ISIC Kaggle Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopy image dataset. Images in this dataset are 1024 × 1024 pixels.

use.training: 750 melanoma and 250 non-melanoma images were allocated for model training.
use.testing: 750 melanoma and 250 non-melanoma images were reserved for testing.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics compared against other methods in tables, but no statistical significance testing was performed or reported.

ISIC Dataset.notes: The proposed system correctly detected 1495 of 1500 melanoma skin images and 1497 of 1500 healthy skin images. The error rate for the ISIC dataset is 1.23%.

SIIM-ISIC Kaggle Dataset.notes: The error rate for the SIIM-ISIC Kaggle dataset is 1.44%.

10-Fold Cross-Validation (Average).notes: Results from 10 experimental runs are averaged. Individual run metrics for SIIM-ISIC Kaggle ranged: Se 98.3-99.3%, Sp 98.6-99.7%, Acc 98.2-99.4%. Individual run metrics for ISIC ranged: Se 96.9-99.7%, Sp 97.9-99.4%, Acc 97.6-99.4%.

architecture_details.backbone: Custom CNN with two modules: Texture Feature Extractor Module (TFEM) and Higher Feature Extractor Module (HFEM)
architecture_details.feature_fusion: Feature Concat (FC) module concatenating TFEM and HFEM outputs

classification_or_output_head.output_description: Biased neurons used for melanoma or non-melanoma classification

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Fully Convolutional Residual Network (FCRN) (Yu et al., 2017)
Regularized Extreme Learning Machine with CNN (S. M. et al., 2023)
Support Vector Machine (SVM) with 2D Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) (Keita et al., 2022)
Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System (ANFIS) (Deivasigamani et al., 2016)
Morphological Snakes (MorphACWE and MorphGAC) (Daghrir et al., 2020)
Visual Geometry Group (VCG) architectures (Naqvi et al., 2023)
Generative Adversarial Neural Networks (GANs) (Dildar et al., 2021)
Satin Bowerbird Optimization (SBO) algorithm (Xu et al., 2020)
Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) algorithm (Khan et al., 2019)
Dual Tree Complex Wavelet Transform (DT-CWT) (Deivasigamani et al., 2016)
preprocessing_techniques: Histogram Equalization (HE) for contrast enhancement and pixel intensity improvement
Gabor transform applied at different orientations, phase shifts, and scales to produce multi-resolution kernel matrices
GLCM (Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix) feature computation for texture differentiation
conclusions: The proposed Gabor transform-based Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) system effectively classifies dermoscopy images as melanoma or non-melanoma, achieving high performance metrics.
On the ISIC dataset, the proposed method achieved an accuracy of 98.58%, sensitivity of 98.66%, and specificity of 98.75%.
On the SIIM-ISIC Kaggle dataset, the proposed method achieved an accuracy of 98.77%, sensitivity of 98.63%, and specificity of 99.11%.

The proposed Gabor-filter-based approach outperformed several existing traditional and deep learning methods, including those by Arvind Kumar Shukla et al. (2025), Abohashish et al. (2025), Al-Rasheed et al. (2023), Khan et al. (2019), and Yu et al. (2017).

The integration of Gabor transforms for multi-resolution texture feature extraction overcomes limitations of conventional CNNs that operate directly in the spatial domain and fail to differentiate low-resolution pixel formats.
The system correctly detected 1495 of 1500 melanoma images and 1497 of 1500 healthy skin images in the ISIC dataset, demonstrating robustness.
The study supports Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) 3 and 4 by facilitating early melanoma detection and enhancing AI-driven medical education.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation, relying solely on two public datasets (ISIC and SIIM-ISIC Kaggle) without testing on independent clinical cohorts or diverse skin tones.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the superiority of the proposed method over comparators.

The dataset split appears non-standard: 1500 melanoma and 1000 non-melanoma images were used, with 750 melanoma and 250 non-melanoma for training, resulting in a significant class imbalance in the training set (3:1 ratio) compared to the test set (3:1 ratio) and the original dataset balance (1:1).

The paper does not report data leakage risks or confirm that patient-level separation was maintained between training and testing sets, which is critical for dermoscopy datasets where multiple images may come from the same patient.

The proposed CNN architecture is custom-built with manually tuned hyperparameters using a Gaussian-Based Optimization Algorithm (GOA), rather than using established, widely-validated backbone architectures, limiting generalizability.
The computational environment is limited to a standard consumer-grade PC (Intel Core i5, 16 GB RAM), which may not reflect performance requirements for clinical deployment on varied hardware.

The paper claims to overcome limitations of conventional CNNs regarding low-resolution pixels but does not provide a detailed ablation study or error analysis on specific image qualities or artifact types (e.g., hair, bubbles).
The 'healthy skin' class is used as the negative class, which may not fully represent the complexity of benign melanocytic lesions often encountered in clinical differential diagnosis.
